data.reasoningText:
Let me create a Blazor Web App for a logistics company that tracks shipments. Based on the requirements:

1. Dispatchers view shipments with status and destination
2. Customers check shipment status and estimated delivery
3. Worldwide users - need to minimize latency
4. Fast first load, then snappy without server round-trips → This suggests Interactive WebAssembly render mode with prerendering
5. Every page has interactive controls
6. Individual accounts authentication

Given the requirements for fast first load (prerendering) + snappy without server round-trips (WebAssembly) + every page interactive, the best render mode is **Interactive WebAssembly** with prerendering enabled. This gives SSR on first load for speed, then WebAssembly takes over for client-side interactivity without server round-trips.


Now I'm setting up the project structure with the Blazor Web App template, configuring it for Interactive WebAssembly render mode globally, and adding individual accounts authentication. I'll need separate server and client projects, with the shipment tracking domain model and dedicated pages for dispatchers and customers.

Blazor Web App (C#)
Author: Microsoft
Description: A project template for creating a Blazor web app that supports both server-side rendering and client interactivity. This template can be used for web apps with rich dynamic user interfaces (UIs).
This template contains technologies from parties other than Microsoft, see https://aka.ms/aspnetcore/11.0-third-party-notices for details.

Usage:
  dotnet new blazor [options] [template options]

Options:
  -n, --name <name>       The name for the output being created. If no name is specified, the name of the output 
                          directory is used.
  -o, --output <output>   Location to place the generated output.
  --dry-run               Displays a summary of what would happen if the given command line were run if it would result 
                          in a template creation. [default: False]
  --force                 Forces content to be generated even if it would change existing files. [default: False]
  --no-update-check       Disables checking for the template package updates when instantiating a template. [default: 
                          False]
  --project <project>     The project that should be used for context evaluation.
  -lang, --language <C#>  Specifies the template language to instantiate.
  --type <project>        Specifies the template type to instantiate.

Template options:
  -f, --framework <net11.0|net10.0|...>    The target framework for the project.
                                           Type: choice
                                             net11.0  
                                             net10.0  Target net10.0
                                             net9.0   Target net9.0
                                             net8.0   Target net8.0
                                           Default: net11.0
  --no-restore                             If specified, skips the automatic restore of the project on create.
                                           Type: bool
                                           Default: false
  --exclude-launch-settings                Whether to exclude launchSettings.json from the generated template.
                                           Type: bool
                                           Default: false
  -int, --interactivity <None|Server|...>  Chooses which interactive render mode to use for interactive components
                                           Type: choice
                                             None         No interactivity (static server rendering only)
                                             Server       Runs on the server
                                             WebAssembly  Runs in the browser using WebAssembly
                                             Auto         Uses Server while downloading WebAssembly assets, then uses 
                                           WebAssembly
                                           Default: Server
  -e, --empty                              Configures whether to omit sample pages and styling that demonstrate basic 
                                           usage patterns.
                                           Type: bool
                                           Default: false
  -au, --auth <Individual|None>            The type of authentication to use
                                           Type: choice
                                             None        No authentication
                                             Individual  Individual authentication
                                           Default: None
  -uld, --use-local-db                     Whether to use LocalDB instead of SQLite. This option only applies if --auth 
                                           Individual is specified.
                                           Type: bool
                                           Default: false
  -ai, --all-interactive                   Configures whether to make every page interactive by applying an interactive 
                                           render mode at the top level. If false, pages will use static server 
                                           rendering by default, and can be marked interactive on a per-page or 
                                           per-component basis.

data.reasoningText:
Now I have a good understanding of the template. Let me build the shipment tracking app. I need to:

1. Create a shared models library (or put models in the client project since both need them)
2. Add Shipment entity to the DB
3. Create API endpoints on the server for shipments
4. Add roles (Dispatcher, Customer)
5. Create pages:
   - Dispatcher dashboard (list shipments, update status)
   - Customer tracking page (check status by tracking number)
6. Update navigation

Let me plan the architecture:
- Shared model classes in Client project (since both server and client reference it)


- Server-side API controllers for CRUD operations
- Client-side pages that call the API via HttpClient

I'll start by creating the Shipment model, setting up the database entity and migrations, building out the API endpoints, then adding the dispatcher dashboard and customer tracking pages, and finally updating the navigation menu.

data.content:
The app is complete and builds successfully. Here's what was created:

---

**ShipmentTracker** — a Blazor Web App with **Interactive WebAssembly** render mode (global) and **Individual accounts** authentication.

### Architecture Choices

| Requirement | Solution |
|---|---|
| Fast first load | Server-side prerendering (SSR) on initial request |
| Snappy after load | WebAssembly takes over — no server round-trips for UI |
| Worldwide latency | All interactivity runs client-side in WASM |
| Interactive controls | Global `InteractiveWebAssembly` render mode |
| Authentication | ASP.NET Identity with roles (Dispatcher/Customer) |

### Key Pages
- **`/`** — Landing page with links to dispatcher dashboard and tracking
- **`/dispatcher`** — (Dispatcher role only) List all shipments, filter by destination, create new shipments, update status via dropdown
- **`/track`** — Public tracking page — enter a tracking number to see status + estimated delivery + progress bar
- **`/myshipments`** — (Authenticated) Shows shipments for the logged-in customer

### Demo Credentials
- **Dispatcher:** `dispatcher@shipmenttracker.com` / `Dispatch1!`
- **Customer:** `customer@example.com` / `Customer1!`

### Run It
```bash
cd ShipmentTracker/ShipmentTracker
dotnet run
```
